_meta.dataPolicy: WSZYSTKIE dane SYNTETYCZNE (przyklad.test, NotkaApp/SklepDemo/PipeDemo, identyfikatory testowe, PESEL placeholder 00000000000). ZERO realnych danych klienta/PII.
_meta.note: Pytania modułu M1. Metadane z master allocation (rozkład globalny). Plik <800 LOC (AGENTS).

questions.learningOutcome: The participant distinguishes a generative task (GenAI) from a classification task (classic ML) based on what the system produces as output.
questions.prompt: A tester works at SklepDemo. Which of the following tasks is a typical example of GenAI (rather than classic ML)?

options.text: Labeling an incoming ticket as 'bug', 'question', or 'request'.
Generating a draft test case based on a requirement description.
Detecting an anomaly in performance metrics relative to a baseline.
Predicting whether a new ticket belongs to the 'urgent' or 'normal' group.

questions.feedbackCorrect: GenAI creates new content (here: the text of a test case draft), whereas the other tasks merely assign a label from a fixed set — that is classic ML.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: Answers A, C, and D assign a label or predict a value from a fixed set — that is classification/prediction (classic ML). GenAI produces new content. Review the GenAI vs. classic ML concept map.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant explains the relationship between LLM and GenAI — that an LLM is a language-specialized subset of GenAI.
questions.prompt: How is the relationship between LLM and GenAI most accurately described?

options.text: LLM and GenAI are two disjoint approaches — an LLM is not part of GenAI.
GenAI is a subset of LLM limited to text generation.
An LLM is a subset of GenAI specialized in language; GenAI also covers generating images or audio.
LLM and GenAI are two names for the same set — every GenAI is an LLM.

questions.feedbackCorrect: An LLM is a language-focused subset of GenAI, while GenAI covers a broader family of content generation (e.g., images), so not every GenAI is an LLM.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: Generating an image for a presentation is GenAI but not an LLM — which proves that GenAI is broader. An LLM is a subset of GenAI, not the other way around. Go back to the concept map (set diagram).

questions.learningOutcome: The participant recognizes that an LLM returns the most probable answer, not a guaranteed truth.
questions.prompt: Which statement best describes the nature of the answer returned by an LLM?

options.text: An LLM searches for and returns a verified fact from a database of true statements.
An LLM predicts the most probable next fragment of text based on patterns from training — which does not guarantee truthfulness.
An LLM always returns the same, single correct answer for a given question.
An LLM checks the answer in real time on the internet before returning it.

questions.feedbackCorrect: An LLM generates the most probable continuation of text according to patterns from training — this is a probabilistic output, so truthfulness is not guaranteed.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: An LLM is neither a truth search engine nor a fact database — it predicts probable text, which is why it can sound credible yet be wrong. Review the 'Where does an LLM's answer come from' screen.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant understands the training vs. inference distinction — that the model does not learn from a single prompt and does not know events after its training date.
questions.prompt: A tester asks the NotkaApp model about an event that happened after the model's training cutoff date and corrects it within a single prompt. What is true here about training and inference?

options.text: The model will permanently remember the correction from this prompt and learn from it for the future.
Inference produces an answer to the current input but does not change the trained patterns — the model does not know events after its training date.
Every use of the model (inference) automatically updates its knowledge about new events.
Training and inference are the same process, so a prompt always trains the model further.

questions.feedbackCorrect: Training establishes patterns once, and inference only generates an answer to your input — a single prompt does not train the model further nor give it knowledge of events after the training date.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: You are confusing training with inference: a correction in a prompt works only in the current conversation; the model does not permanently remember it. Review the 'Training vs. inference' screen.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant assigns a synthetic QA task to the correct one of four solution categories (LLM / classic ML / rule-based system / human expert).
questions.prompt: Match each synthetic QA task to the most appropriate solution category.

pairs.left: Write a draft of 5 test cases for the login requirement in NotkaApp
Label an incoming ticket as 'bug', 'question', or 'request'
Check whether a postal code has the format NN-NNN
Decide whether to hold a release due to legal risk

questions.feedbackCorrect: Each task landed in the category that matches the nature of its output: text generation (LLM), a label from a fixed set (ML), an explicit deterministic rule (rule-based), accountable judgment (human).
questions.feedbackIncorrect: Pay attention to what the task produces: new content = LLM, a label from a fixed set = classic ML, a fully predictable result per a rule = rule-based system, a decision carrying accountability = human. Review the 'Task classifier' screen.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant matches a concept from the GenAI/LLM foundations to its correct definition.
questions.prompt: Match each concept from the GenAI/LLM foundations to its correct definition.

pairs.right: Artificial intelligence that creates new content (text, image, code)
A language model that predicts the most probable next fragment of text
A model that learns a pattern and assigns a label or predicts a value
An output that sounds credible but is not grounded in data

questions.feedbackCorrect: The definitions align with the concept map: GenAI creates, classic ML classifies/predicts, an LLM predicts probable text, and a hallucination is a credible-sounding yet ungrounded output.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: You mixed up at least one concept — the key distinction is 'creates' (GenAI) vs. 'classifies' (ML), and a hallucination is about a lack of grounding, not an error in form itself. Review the concept map and the probabilistic-output screen.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant recognizes a deterministic task and chooses a rule-based solution instead of an LLM, where a probabilistic output is undesirable.
questions.prompt: At SklepDemo, you need to fully automatically check whether an order number has a valid format (e.g., ORD-TEST-777) — the result must be identical and unambiguous every time. Which solution is most appropriate?

options.text: An LLM with the prompt 'assess whether the order number is valid' — because it understands context.
A rule-based system with an explicit format rule (e.g., a regular expression) — a deterministic and repeatable result.
A classic ML model trained on examples of numbers to predict validity.
An LLM, but asked to explain its decision to increase confidence.

questions.feedbackCorrect: Format validation is a deterministic task — the same input always yields the same result per an explicit rule, so a rule-based system is reliable and repeatable, while an LLM's probabilistic output is an unnecessary risk here.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: An LLM returns a probabilistic output, so it may give different answers for the same number — which disqualifies it for a deterministic task. Review the task types: deterministic tasks belong to a rule-based system.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant decides that an LLM's output requires verification before it is used as a fact — because it is probabilistic and may be a hallucination.
questions.prompt: An LLM generated a summary of the NotkaApp requirements and claims: 'An account lock lasts 24 hours'. The tester only knows that such output is sometimes made up. What is the safest decision?

options.text: Accept the statement as a fact — the model sounds confident and consistent.
Verify the statement against the source requirement before use, because an LLM's output is probable but not guaranteed.
Reject the entire summary as useless, since one sentence may be wrong.
Ask the same model whether it is sure — and trust its confirmation.

questions.feedbackCorrect: An LLM's output is probabilistic and may be a hallucination, so a numeric claim must be verified against the source before it serves as a fact — that is the correct, cautious QA decision.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: Trusting the model's confident tone (A, D) ignores the nature of probabilistic output, and wholesale rejection (C) is an overreaction — verification is needed for the specific, checkable statement. Review the probabilistic-output and hallucination screen.
